JAMES STEPHENS.

By kind permission of Messrs., Macmillan we are enabled this month
| to present to our readers a portrait of Mr. James Stephens, who has

1 Jeapt into fame as a prose writer in a manuer unequalled by any other

_ﬁ °  Irishman since the days of Goldsinith. Yet verse, as a medium for
| °  his message was Mr. Stephen’s first essay, and it is an unfailing
| = source of pleasure to us that we were one of the first of the literary

{  journals to hail himn as a new singer, which we did in our second num-
! ] “ber. Since then Mr. Stephens has gone on from success to success, and
I critics of all sorts have nothing but praise for his works. Here is a
in the front rank of literary criticism—says of his latest book:—** Mr.
i Stephens is undoubtedly a new power among the writers of Ireland, and
r it would be idle to deny that his nationality and his genius are things
| inseparable. In a country of continuous literary traditions he would
“have heen cramped by precedent and prejudice, with the result that his
finest fancies must have been either suppressed or allowed to appear as
brilliant affectations. But, in a land little given to the arts of peace
~during the centuries of which we have knowledge, a wealth of unex-
“ pressed thought may accumulate, waiting only for those who have the
wit to discover it and the sympathy to use it aright. In all that 18
. Mow being written in Ireland, and of all that has been written during
: the last ten or twenty years, the best and the most memorable part is
rich from the lives and the genuine traditions of the people.
Wo know all about the notebooks of Synge, and honour him tho
., ¢ moro for having used them, for a man need not manufacture paints in
order to call himself a croative artist, but Mr. Stephons is not to-be
ccounted quite in this way. His inspiration is as much in him as
f ‘ahouy him, and this at least is corbain that ho has been guided by mo
"i:""l meroly literary impulse, whother Irish, English, or French. Ho is
.3': the interpreber of tho raco less from study than from indigenousy under-

Ao

standing. So it comes that he reveals the things which haye e
hidden since a time only reached by legends, and we see that they ary |
auncient but not rusted, fresh but not with any hint of crudeness, is 5

not in a land grown old in literary usage to produce this kind of work, i3

and in the new nations it would be inconceivable since their people ey
straight from boyhood to the growing of beards. Ifor the races of oy
speech, this touching of to-day with the whole past can only be pos. |
sible 1n Ireland, but it is surely a satisfaction that Mr. Stephens gy
the rest have arisen with the power to put all this into the languag, g
of the wider countries, and to do it without distortion of the origin] i
thought. It is a far greater thing for the world than any Provenc
poet has accomplished.

-y,

Had anything resembling this book been produced in circumstances i@ ,
other than those we have outlined, it would have seemed to be the work :
of an amazingly clever writer, and the fear would have been present E
that it represented all the virtue that was in him, but there is small .
need for such a thought, and clever is not the word which describes 1
Mr. Stephens. Possibly it might be applied to his handling of words, %
but never to his thoughts, for in these is the wisdom which has never
been taught, and we know that this comes from an ancient spring,
which is not to be exhausted in the span of life. He will write again,

o
"

and his future work will be very good, though mone can say how gool
since there are no standards by which we can speak of him as a new
planet arisen, for the old phrase conveys too little, and would suggest
even that he was one of the many and a borrower of light. Nothing
could be further from the truth; he stands alone, and reflects from m
known sun. He is an interpreter, but of that only which by birth wis
always in himself. Of tho mystery and the wisdom of his book thi

little can be said : to his humour we have given the only possible tribute
of the laughter of pure delight.”

And ‘“‘The Academy,” swelling the mnote of praise, concludes
equally eulogistic article by saying:—* We can scarcely imagine any
one reading this hook without having a sunshine kindled in his soul;
and without looking with a kindlior and wiser eye on his dear Mother
Barth and his Brother Man. And if Mr. Ste phens wants better praise
than that, then he is a different man from what we have taken him for."

It is surely a groat gratification to know that to Ireland the literary
world owes the three most ori ginal creative writers of the prosent cel
tury—Synge, Shaw, and Stephens.

THE IRISH BOOK LOVER. 07

NIRST IRISH PAPERS.
(Vol. IV., p. 61.)

With reference to this exceedingly interesting  extract
from the ‘‘Times” appearing as above, I would like to correct
one or two errors. It 1s not true that nothing was printed in Cork
antil the Cromwellian Army occupied that city. T lately contributed
to the Royal Irish Academy a list of books and pamphlets printed in
Cork during the 17th century, and from that list it will appear that
there were fifteen 1tems printed 1in Cork before Cromwell occupied that
town. It 1s also stated that there was mo printer in Ireland save Wmn.
Bladen in 1641, but I am inclined to question that statement. There
is not sufficient evidence to be positive on the point. Thomas Bourke
was printing in 1643 in Waterford, and there is stated to have been
printing in Kilkenny in 1642. Besides, Bladen was Stake Printer, and
o had much public printing to do. There may have been others print-
ing privately. Peter de Pienne, a Cork printer, was not printing in
Iteland in 1641 perhaps, but he was in the years 1648-9 and later.

As regards the publication of newspapers in the 17th century the
writer of the paragraph in the ‘Times’’ probably had not seen the
contribution which I made some years ago on the subject to the ‘‘ In-
dependent,” in which I gave a list of the papers or periodicals published
for that century in Dublin, and therefor he has omitted a few. The
following are the journals so far as I was able to make them out as
published in Dublin in the 17th century.

1. An Account of the General Occurrences in Ireland, together with
some particulars from England. A weekly journal, appearing in 1660.

2. Mercurius Hibernicus, or the Irish Intelligencer, 1663.

3. The Dublin Newsletter, 1685, and perhaps a year earlier, and
continued down to 1686.

4. The Dublin Intelligencer, 1690, printed by Jos. Ray, continued
down to September, 1693.

5. The Present Stato of Burope, or the Historical and Political
Monthly Mercuny, giving an account of all the public occurrences.
Printed hy Andrew Orook, commencing 1690 and lasting 3 or 4 years.

6. Tho TFlying Post, or the Post Master, printed by Carter, Fish-
amble Street, 1699. It also appeared in 1700.

7. An Tdition of the London Gazotte, printed by Carter, in Decem-

ber, 1700.

98 THE IRISH BOOK LOVIER.

The article to which this is a reply refers to the Dublin Gagzety,
printed in tha reign of King James II., and in the Irish Builder, VQ];
XIX., p. 184, it is stated that Pue’s Occurrences appeared first in De.
cember, 1700. Of course, this may be a mistake for 1703.

I might also mention that the paper entitled *‘ Votes of the Hoygg
of Commons,”” 1692, Nos. 1-13, appears in my list of Dublin printeg
books of the 17th century, p. 251. Therefore its publication had Begy
noticed.

Perhaps these corrections are hardly worth being made, but still 1
make them for the information of the readers of the ‘‘Irish Book
Lover.” E. R. McC. DIX,

J. C. LYONS AND THE LEDESTOWN PRESS.

In vol. I., p. 69, Mr. James Tuite, of Mullingar, gave a graphic ac-
count of Mr. John Charles Lyons and his private printing press at
Ledestown. It has been my good fortune to obtain a copy of another
work that issued from that press which has hitherto been unrecorded.
It is a pamphlet in wrappers printed in two colours, 83 by 5%, title
page -+ 206 pp., folds 1in fours, entitled ‘° Historical Notice, etc., of the
Family of Lyons, and its connexions. Noli Irritare Leones. Ledes-
town : Printed by John Charles Lyons, 1853.”” It is inscribed ‘¢ E. Wal-
ford, from Mr. Lyons,” and contains an interesting autograph letter
which is worth transcribing.

Ledestown,
Mullingar, Ireland.
Ist July, 1853.

Sir,—In 1853 I published a rather extonsive work on the Grand
Juries of the Co. of Westmeath, since the year 1727, interspersed with
anecdotes of the families. I send you a copy of it, as you are related
to the family of Lyons and its connexions. You will seo all in it you
may require, and may depend on its correctness. I was myself educated
at Reading, in Berkshire, under that best of scholans and most amiable
of men, Revd. Dr. Richard Valpy, and was afterwards at Oxford, bub
from having heen heir to a great estate, being idle and most delicate
in health, did not remain sufliciently to take any degree.

Your Obedient Servant,
- JOHN LYONS-

THE IRISH BOOK LOVER. 99

mpe work itself abounds in anecdotos, with some of which I may amuse
vour readers at some future date., He gives a few fresh facts concern-
;ng himself and family that may be of interest. * He obtained a com-
pany in the Westmeath Militia in 1815, which, with the other regiments
of Irish Militia, was disembodied in (6th April) 1816. He married 14th
March, 1820, Penelope Melesina, only daughter of the late Hugh Tuite,
of Sonna, by whom he has issue. (She died 10th February, 1855.) 1.,
Charles, born 7th February, 1821 (died 1859, 24th February). II., Mary
Ann Melesina, married in 1848, James Malley, solicitor, of the City of
Dublin, by whom she has i1ssuc. Mr. Lyons was appointed a magistrate
in 1814 (1s D.L. of the Co. V. Meath), and was chief magistrate of Mul-
lingar from 19th March, 1817, to the death of the LEarl of Granard in
1836, since which period the office has not been renewed. (He married
secondly, 12th November, 1856, Francis Ellen Walsh, of Belle Vue, Co.
Westmeath, by whom he had issue, a dau., Caroline Constance.) The
passages 1n brackets are added in Lyons’ handwriting. The types
used in the book are mompareil, hourgeois, italics and large and small
capitals. The workmanship is excellent—mnot in the least amateurish.
Edward Walford (1823-1879) to whom this copy was presented was the
well-known historian, genealogist and bookman.

And now Mr. Tuite writes recently, ‘‘ You will be surprised to learn
that I have secured possession of the printing press which J. O. Lyons,
of Ledes'town, used for printing his books. He made it himself in 18283,
and his name is engraved on the steecl socket over the platten. It 1s
in Tight good condition. I purchased it from the proprietor of the
“Westmeath Guardian,” Mr. J. Farrell, whose father-in-law, the late
proprietor of the ‘* Guardian,”’ purchased it at the auction at Ledes-
town shortly after Lyon’s death. . . I am very proud to have this

relic of a very interesting man.” J.S.C.

IRISH LITERARY SOCIETY.

On Wednesday, 4th December, Miss Jane Hardy entertained mem-
bers and visitors withi a Tea Table Talk on Felicia Hemans, to whom
she wag relatod by ties of hlood. She insisted strongly on the Irish
origin of the poetess, whose father, George Browne, was a native of
Dublin and connected with the family of the Marquis of Sligo, a fact
“ncealed hy her sister in her memoir and naturally unknown to other
biografphere. Miss Hardy rapidly sketched the lifo of her subject, her

triumphs and her trials, her relationship with Scott and \\-’01’(15“'01'1;],,
hor eallant struggle to maintain and educate her children, when g
strangely deserted by her husband. The last four years of her life were
spont in Dublin, and Miss Hardy read Sir W. H. Russell s reminiscenceg
of the poetess, who was his near neighbour, from his recently publisheq
¢“ Tife.”” She died at the age of 41, and was Dburied in St. Anne's
Church, close to the house in which she resided, and a small tabhlet
therein records the fact. Another tablet was erected to her menory
in St. Asaph’s Cathedral, in Wales, near which her happy early years
were spent. Miss Hardy said the poetic talent was strong in all the
collateral branches of the Brownes, and ridiculed the 1idea that the
poetess derived her lyric genius from her mother, a woman of mixed
[talian and German descent. An interesting feature of the occasion
was the exhibition of several portraits, autographs and relics of the
poetess, and her relations. A brief discussion followed, and in convey-
ing the customary vote of thanks the Chairman, Dr. Crone, said Mrs.
Hemans had left half-a-dozen lyries that the world would not willingly
let die.

On Saturday, 7th December, with Surgeon General Evatt, C.B., in
the chair, Mrs. E. M. Field, the author of ** Iithne’ and other well
known stories, delivered a lecture on ‘‘ William Bedell, Bishop of Kil-
more,’”” whose funeral procession, in 1642—Englishman and Protestant
though he was—included a mourning crowd of natives, and the rebel
soldiery fired a volley over his grave. Deep piety, great organising
power, and self-sacrificing diligence were joined in him to a breadth
of sympathy and an insight unusual in his day. To his early experiences
in Venice, his close friendship with Paolo Sarpi, and his intercourse
with the numerous educated Jews there, his understanding of alien
minds was largely due. {In 1627 he came over as Provost of T.C.D.,
and there instituted many reforms, insisting on Irish studies, ve-
organising the finances, and leaving the disordered statutes re-written
in his own hand in excellent Latin. Raised to the bishopric of Kilmore
in 1629, ho continued zealous for reforms, abolishing pluralities, reno-
vating churches, insisting upon sound learning in his incumbents, and
a knowledge of the vernacular. He laboured for the good of all, and
made vigorous protests against tho oppression of the poor Catholics
to the frequent displeasure of Strafford and others in high places. His
translation of the Soriptures into Irish is still the best. When the
troubles of 1641 broke out he fed and sholtered some 200 people in his

THE [RISH BOOK LOVER. 101

pome, and 1t is believed that his imprisonment in Loughoughter Castle
gas in great measure an cffort of the rebel leaders for his protection.
Though succoured by the Sheridans and O’Reilly’s, he died of illness
prought on by the hardships of his confinement that winter. His
requiem cannot be better expressed than by the Catholic priest who
said, by his grave-side, ‘** Sit anima mea cum Bedello!”’

Rev. Mr. Stanford, a descendant of the Bishop, moved a vote of
thanks, and pointed out that Bedell was able to preach in Italian and
Irish, and Prof. Mahafly had declared he did more for T.C.D. in
eichteen months than his predecessor had done in eighteen years.

Bibliography :—1ILafe, by Bishop Burnett, 8vo., Lond., 1635; Dublin,
1736. By H. J. Monck Mason, Lond., 1843. By Alexander Clogy,
Lond., 1862, Lecture, by Rt. Hon. J. Napier, Dublin, 1863. Life,
J. E. B. Mayor, Cambridge, 1871. By T. Wharton Jones, 4to., Lond.,
1872. Two Biographies, ed. by Ii. S. Shuckburgh, Camb., 1902.

On December 8th an informal dinner was held at the Osborne Hotel,
Tavistock Place. Dr. Evans Wentz, who secured a literary degree at
Rheims and a science degree at Oxford, for his thesis, since published,
proving the existence of fairies, was the guest of the evening. The
table was decorated with fairy bells, fairy horns and fairy dolls, while
songs dealing with ‘‘ the little people” were sung by Miss Moya Herd-
man and Miss Perry. Miss Ethel Rolt Wheeler, author of ‘‘ Famous
Blue Stockings,’”’ occupied the chair.

AN IRISH-AMERICAN WRITER—EDMUND BAILEY
O’CALLAGHAN.

This well-known author of historical works, pertaining to New York

State and City, was born in Mallow, Co. Cork, in 1797.  Spent two

yoars in Paris, and left his native town for Canada in 1823. Studied
medicine at Quebec, and, after a few ycars practice at his profession
in thig city, removed to Montreal, where hoe edited a newspaper called
“The Vindicator”’ from 1834-37. Was olected membor of the Legis-
lature, but on account of his prominent participation in the Canadian
troubles of 1837, had to fly to tho United States, a large roward being
offerod for his apprehension. Settled down in Albany and practised as
doctor for a short time. Appointed custodian of the Historical Manu-
Bcripts in tho Secrotary of Stato Office. This position ho held till 1870,
when ho removod to New York. His library contained a large collec-

tion of rare books and MSS. He was a ‘member of the Historicy} -
Society of New York, M.D. of the University of St. Louis, and LLD
of St. John’s College, Fordham. His death took place at his residence
No. 651 Lexington Avenue, New Yof]{, on the 29th of May, 188
Cardinal McOluskey, Bishops Lynch and Conroy, and other distinguisheg
ecclesiastics, as well as several prominent citizens of New York ang
Albany, attended his obsequies at St. Pa-‘triokl’a Cathedral. He wag
buried in QOalvary Cemetery. 3

BIBLIOGRAPHY.
1. History of the New Netherlands.
9. Jesuit Relations of Discoveries. ;
3, Documentary History of New York. Illustrated with maps,
portraits, etc. 4 vols. Albany, 1830. |
4. Documents relating to the Colonial History of New York.
5. Commissary Wilson’s Orderly Book.
6. Orderly Book of General John Burgoyne. From original MSS.
Albany, 1860.
7. Journal of the Legislative Assemblies of the State of New York.
8. American’ Bibles. With Introductory and Bibliographical
Notes. Albany, 1861.
9. The Register of the New Netherlands (1626-1674).
10. Voyages of George Clarke to Ameriza.
12. Historical MSS. relating to the War of the Revolution.
13. Laws and Ordinances of the New. Netherlands.
14. Proceedings of the Common Council of New York. (Not pub-
lished.) "
Nearly all the foregoing works were issued from the press of Joel
Munsell, Albany, hefore the author’s removal to New York.
Leeds. W.H.

GOSSIP,

I wish to call attention to an accidental transposition at top of p- -
8l. Sir T. N. Fitzgerald was the matural philosopher, and Fitzgibbon
the lord justice, as everyone knows now, but if uncorrected might lead
to misapprchension in the years to come.

Mr. H. R. Plomer, in an interesting article in ‘‘ The Library,”’ to]la
us that Thomas Cranley, Archbishop of Dublin (1391-1417), lord jus-

-

e iy LY el
¥ xgtl_ _L‘. ,_,i —i,‘! ol —

s

THE IRISH BOOK LOVER. 103

tice and chancellor, ‘“‘was a great book-collector, and many of his
ireasured volumes are still on the shelves of New College, Oxford.”
He was something of a poet, too, for he expressed his complaints
against the mere Irish in a poetical epistle to the king, consisting of
no less than 106 verses.

Sir Wilham Osler, equally eminent as a book lover and physician,
recently addressed the Royal Society of Medicine on Sir William Petty
and the Down Survey, founding his observations on a MS. account
which he had discovered in two volumes of Petty’s letters which he
had purchased at the Phillipps sale in 1911. Petty, who founded the

~noble house of Lansdowne, was originally a professor of anatomy, and

surveyed the estates confiscated by Cromwell. I wonder if that MS.
contained the confirmation of the old story that where no owner or
claimant was forthcoming, the name of ‘‘ Petty ”’ was wntten ‘‘ down.”

I am sorry to hear of the death, on December 6th, of Dr. Bernard,
of Buncrana, in his 85th year. One of the few surviving surgeons who
served in the Crimea, he settled in Derry soon afterwards. He took
a great interest in local antiquities, and to him we owe the restoration
of the Grinan of A'i:l-each,-in Innishowen, that wonderful cyclopean
structure of which we have a continuous record from the third century.
I was glad to see, on visiting it last year, that it had been taken over
as a national monument by the Board of Works. Some years ago when
I was compiling a bibliography of Derry, Dr. Bernard lent me ready
assistance. He had published an account of Aileach and its restoration,
reprinted from the Journal R.S.A.I., ¢ Jottings from my Note Book,”
and many medical papers.

Irish readers of the December magazines will turn first to a char-
acteristic little comedy in one act entitled ‘‘ The Bogey Men,” by Lady
Gregory, in the ‘‘TFortnightly,” and then to a sympathetic article on
“Father George Tyrell,” by A. F. Bell. In the * Contemporary *’
there is anothior article on Father Tyrell more biographical than the
Previous one, from the pen of Canon Lilley, an Ulsterman who upholds
the Irigh reputation for pulpit oratory in the me tropolis.

In the *Nineteenth Century?’ Dr. R. H. Murray replies to tho

recent article by J. B. Williams, on ‘' Cromwell ab Drogheda,’”’ to
Which T directed special attention on its appearance & couple of months
ago. Dr. Murray recites again the story of the ™ Bloody Bridge ™
(whilst admitting that many of the T.C.D. depositions ™ are ex aggerat-
¢d and some untrue’’), and holds that it was Cromwell’s ‘‘ work to

avengo the slaughtered saints of God, and it would have been useleg,

to point out that there was no proof that the soldiers of Drogheda wep,

Sir Hugh Clifford has a fine appreciation of Sir Wm. Butler in tjq

““ Cornhill,” to which also Miss W. M. Letts contributes a poem ep.

titled ‘‘ Voices,”” whilst ‘* The Irish Review,” opening with a fine son.

net by W. B. Yeats, has a neat little poem by Con O’Leary, ‘ The

Fugitive,” and a clever sketch by Dermot O’Byrne, ‘‘ The Call of the. .

Road,” amongst other anteresting articles.

In the journal R.S.A.I. Mr. Westropp continues his excellent work
descriptive of the Coast IForts of Mayo; Mr. H. S. Crawford has a
lengthy list of Early Cross Slabs and Pillars; and Dr. Grattan Flood
gives a lengthy description of ‘‘ Barton’s Psalms, the carliest known
Dublin-printed Music Book.”

One of the handsomest calendars I have seen 1s that 1ssued by Wm.
Marshall & Co., Victoria Street, Belfast., It contains short biographies
and excellent portraits of Moore, Lady DBlessington, Father Prout,
Lever, FFather Mathew, and Lord Dufferin. It would be a useful orna-
ment in any Irish houschold.

REVIEWS.

Tre JourNAL oF JonN STEVENS containing a Brief Account of the
War in Ireland, 1689-91. Edited by the Rev. R. H. Murray, Litt.D.

(Oxford, Clarendon Press.) The MS. here printed in its entirety for

the first time has heen in the British Museum since 1899, when it was

purchased at the sale of the Tixall library, where it had rested for &

hundred and thirty years. Thero is another version amongst the
Phillipps MSS. which is utilized by Von Ranke, the great German his-
torian, who married a sistor of Bishop Graves, of Limerick., It is 8
Jjournal of the greatest interest and value, commencing, with the flight
of James II., and breaking off abruptly in the midst of a description of
the Battle of Aughrim. It is the production of a keen observer and
a ready writer—the author of soveral works. Amn adheront of James,

whoso loyalty to his lawful sovereign no thing—poverty, hardship, of |

unmerited slights—could quench. A peacoful citizen, he followed

James to France, voluntecred for the campaign in Ireland, served &b

THE IRISH BOOK LOVER. 105

the Boyue, Athlone, the first siege of Limerick and Aughrim. A faith-
ful recorder, he exposes the seamy side of affairs, and draws a fear-
ful picture of the vice rampant in Dublin during the winter of 1689-90,
only hinted at by O’Kelly. He describes the terrible devastation of
the country, the jealousies which divided the allies, the raising of the
raw levies, the peculations of the officers, and denounces the incapa-
city and cowardice of his own men. He abhors the *‘ brass money”’
«ith all the fervour of an alderman of the unreformed corporation, and
groans over the length of the Irish miles. Altogether a human docu-
ment; a vivid presentation, and one of the richest additions to his-
torical literature of recent times. The editor, Dr. Murray, has done
his part well. The introduction and notes evince a wide knowledge of
the authorities of the period both in print and MS., and there i1s an
excellent and up-to-date bibliography. We only notice one slight shp.
In a note on p. 165 *‘ the sixth Duke of Abercorn” should, of course,
be ‘‘ Earl.”

THoMAS ANDREWS, SHIrBuILDER. By Shan . Bullock. (Maunsel.)
‘“Mr, Shan Bullock . . . has done no better work than in this tribute
to one of the noblest Irishmen Ulster has produced in modern times.”
So says Sir Horace Plunkett in the introduction to this account of the
short but strenuous life and heroic death of Tom Andrews, which has
been compiled from the recollections of those wlo had known him in
his youth, the ‘‘pals’” who had worked beside him in his manhood,
and those who witnessed his self-sacrificing conduct in the last awful
hours when the ‘ Titanic’’ plunged headlong to her doom. No one
who reads the record can gainsay the dictum of Sir Horace, and we
hope that every Irish boy will be encouraged to stucdy this brief but
heautiful life story of ‘‘a man who died for men.”

MyLEs SwWEENEY. In the ‘‘ Shamrock’ of 4th Aprl, 1874, there 15

Clare’s Yellow Dragoons.” Myles is stated to have been son of Lord |
Clare’s gamekeeper, and to have fought in the Williamite war and §
under Clare on the continent. He was present at Pultowa, and spent bf;
some time as a prisoner in Siberia. ;

formation or direct me to historical material referring to him. He is
not mentioned by O’Callaghan,

WinLtaya Toop Jones. (Vol. IV., p. 73.) The ‘‘Dublin Journal”
of 27th I'ebruary, 1818, has this obitua ry notico: ‘ Died on Tuesday,
17th inst., W. T. Jones, of Rostrovor, sq., formerly M.P. for Lisburn,
his native town. This respected gentleman had , on the preceding Sun-
day, dined withl Mr. Martin, of Kilbronoy. In tho evening ho had just
stept into his carriago, accompanied by Dr. John Bingham and a young
clergyman, to return to Rostroevor., Unhappily the horses suddenly

Dublin. AM.T.

William Todd Jones died at Rostrevor on 14th February, 1818, aged

63; he was therefore born about 1755. He claimed descent from Jeremy
Taylor, and supplied Heber with the story that ‘‘about a century”
after Taylor’s death his bones, with those of George Rust, his successor,
were removed from the vault in the cathedral of Dromore, to make
room for the coffin of another bishop; but were replaced by bishop
Thomas Percy. This unsupported story is shown by Mant to be wholly
incredible, See art. Taylor, Jeremy, in D.N.B., with authorities there
given.

London, A.G.

GavtoyoziNy. IFFrom April till July, 1779, a series of very able let-
ters on Irish affairs appeared in the ‘‘ Freeman’s Journal’ over the
signatures of ‘‘ Gautimozin’”’ and ‘‘Causidicus.” They were issued in
a volume by Henry and Robert Joy, Belfast, 1779, and the title-page
states that the letters had Dbeen reprinted in London and had gone
through several editions there. Is it known who the writers were?

Belfast. WILLIAM MAYES.

we find the Lord Lieutenant, the Marquis of Buckingham, informing

in the press, and to secure his services to Government by a pension of
£300 a year during his own and his wife’s lifetime.”

SIR RoBERT ANDERsON’S ‘‘ HisTorY oF FFENIANISM,” (?) In the in-
teresting controversy carried on in your pages in May, June and July,
1910, in which the ‘‘ Daily News,” ‘‘The Star,” and the ¢ Freeman’s
Journal ”’ joined, regarding Sir Robert Anderson’s admission in ¢ Black-
wood’s Magazime’ for March of that year, that: ‘‘Torty years ago

I published the secret history of the FFenian movement up
to date.” In an interview with a ‘““Star” reporter, Sir Robert said
this had appeared in the ‘‘ Contemporary Review,”’ 1868-69. A cor-
respondent (vol. I., p. 169) informed you that he had searched a file of
the Review for thoso years, and failed to find it, hinted at a lapse of

memory, and asked had any other reader heen more fortunate. Up t0

the present no one has replied. Some of your readers may therefor

<care to know that I happened by accident on the articles in quostion in
the ‘ Contemporary Review ”’ for March and April, 1872, under the
title of ‘‘ I'enianism—A. Narrative.”

As it was my simplo query regarding the authorship of *‘ The Seeret
History of the I'onian Movement,” by John Rutherford, that gtarted

THE IRISH BOOK LOVER. 109

ihe controversy and led to the unravelling of that mystery, I hope I
may be allowed to place on record this further discovery—another of

ihe literary mysteries that have heen solved by I.B.L. and its readers.

May I add that the ‘‘ narrative ”” seems to me to be a fair and im-
partial account, as witness the following paragraph: ¢ One feature of
the ‘rising’ has mever received the prominence to which it is entitled,
and that 1s the fewness of the wanton outrages committed by the rebels.
Although some districts were entirely at their mercy during the night
of the 5th of March, such outrages were not the rule, but the rare ex-
ception ; and these were the work of the American desperadoes, strang-
ers in the country. This fact deserves to be recorded to the credit of
the Irish people. Without entering here upon the probable causes to
which this result was due, it may be noticed in passing that the out-
break was free from the element of religious rancour which embittered

the rebellion of 1798.”’
Belfast. A. A. CAMPBELL.

UxruBLisHED HisTory or FFErRMaNaGcH. The mention of ** Premium ”’
Madden in the current number of I.B.L. reminds me that amongst his
numerous literary undertakings was a history of this county, wherein
he spent his long and useful life. From a communication in ‘‘ Notes
and Queries ”’ for October, 1879, it would appear that the MS. was at
that date in the library of Sir Thomas Phillips at Thirlestane House,
Cheltenham, It was described as a small 4to of 165 pages, written
1718-19, entitled *“ History of the County of Fermanagh with the Antient
Families of the Same.” The contents included a description of the
town of Enniskillen, the islands, hills and mountains of the county ;
the old families such as Maguires, MacManuses, Cassidys, etc., and
the principle British families. As the contents of Phillipps’ library
have heen dispersed it would be interesting to know the present rest-
ing place of this history for no county in Ireland has been less written
about than Ifermanagh.
